did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning-based smartphone application utilizing a DenseNet-161 convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture to diagnose basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and other skin diseases from clinical images. The AI model was trained on 17,784 clinical images and its performance (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, AUC) was evaluated and compared against dermatologists and non-dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is labeled as a 'Correspondence' but presents original experimental results from a study comparing a deep learning-based application against dermatologists and non-dermatologists. It details the generation of an AI-driven smartphone application using a DenseNet-161 CNN, training on 17,784 images, and reporting new performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, AUC) derived from a survey of 36 dermatologists and 14 non-dermatologists. Although published as a correspondence, it functions as a research paper reporting original validation data.

title: Performance of a deep learning-based application for the diagnosis of basal cell carcinoma in Indian patients as compared to dermatologists and nondermatologists
brief_summary: This study developed and evaluated a deep learning-based smartphone application using a DenseNet-161 convolutional neural network to diagnose basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and other skin diseases on Indian skin. The model was trained on 17,784 clinical images sourced from public archives and dermatologists across India. Performance was assessed via an online survey involving 36 dermatologists, 5 surgeons, and 9 general physicians using 58 biopsy-proven BCC images mixed with 42 non-BCC lesions. The application achieved a sensitivity of 80.24%, specificity of 91.57%, and accuracy of 84.97%, with an AUC of 0.933. The app's sensitivity and accuracy were significantly higher than those of both dermatologists and non-dermatologists, while its specificity was comparable to dermatologists. The authors conclude that the app can effectively aid physicians, particularly general practitioners, in screening for BCC in settings with limited dermatological expertise.

all_task_types.target: Diagnosis of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) versus 39 other skin diseases (non-BCC lesions)
all_task_types.details: The model performs binary classification (BCC vs. non-BCC) and multi-class classification (diagnosing among 40 skin diseases). The app outputs the top three probabilities for disease diagnosis. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and AUC for distinguishing BCC from benign lesions and other skin cancers.

datasets_used.name: Training Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of 17,784 clinical images sourced from public archives (HellenicDermAtlas, Danderm) and dermatologists across India. It included 871 Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) images and images representing 39 other skin diseases (non-BCC lesions).

use.training: Used to train the DenseNet-161 CNN model for diagnosing BCC and 39 other skin diseases.
use.other: Images were sourced from public archives (http://www.hellenicdermatlas.com/en and http://www.danderm.dk/atlas) and dermatologists across India.

datasets_used.name: Evaluation Survey Dataset
datasets_used.description: A set of 100 clinical images used for testing the application's performance against human experts. It consisted of 58 biopsy-proven BCC images randomly mixed with 42 images of facial non-BCC lesions (confirmed by histopathology or consensus diagnosis by two dermatologists).

use.testing: Used in an online survey via Google Forms to evaluate the app's performance compared to 36 dermatologists and 14 non-dermatologists (5 surgeons, 9 general physicians). The app was also tested on these images by three different investigators.

statistical_methods_used.method: Two-tailed Student’s t test with Welch correction
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate statistical significance (P values) comparing the sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy of the deep learning application against dermatologists, surgeons, and general physicians.
To plot and visualize the performance of the app model, specifically to determine the Area Under the Curve (AUC) for distinguishing BCC from non-BCC lesions.
To report the summary statistics for sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy for the app, dermatologists, surgeons, and general physicians.

hypothesis_testing.test: Two-tailed Student’s t test with Welch correction
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in sensitivity, specificity, or accuracy between the deep learning application and the human experts (dermatologists, surgeons, or general physicians).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in sensitivity, specificity, or accuracy between the deep learning application and the human experts.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The app's sensitivity and accuracy were significantly higher than both dermatologists (P < 0.0001) and nondermatologists (app vs. surgeons, P < 0.0001; app vs. GPs, P < 0.0001). The specificity of the app was comparable to that of dermatologists (P = 0.0704).

Evaluation Survey Dataset (58 BCC + 42 N.notes: Statistical significance was calculated using two-tailed Student’s t test with Welch correction. The app's sensitivity and accuracy were significantly higher than both dermatologists (P < 0.0001) and nondermatologists (app vs. surgeons, P < 0.0001; app vs. GPs, P < 0.0001). The specificity of the app was comparable to that of dermatologists (P = 0.0704).

classification_or_output_head.details: Outputs top three probabilities for disease diagnosis

conclusions: The deep learning-based smartphone application (using a DenseNet-161 CNN) achieved a sensitivity of 80.24%, specificity of 91.57%, and accuracy of 84.97% in diagnosing basal cell carcinoma (BCC) on Indian skin.
The app's sensitivity and accuracy were significantly higher than those of both dermatologists (P < 0.0001) and non-dermatologists (surgeons and general physicians, P < 0.0001).
The app's specificity was comparable to that of dermatologists (P = 0.0704).
The area under the curve (AUC) for the app model was 0.933, which was comparable to previously reported AUCs in similar studies.
The app demonstrated high top-3 sensitivity (96.6%), indicating it included the correct diagnosis within its top three probability outputs frequently.
The application can effectively differentiate BCC from benign lesions and other skin cancers in clinical images.
The app can be directly used by physicians, particularly general practitioners, using ordinary smartphones to screen for BCC in a timely and cost-effective manner.

App augmentation may empower general practitioners to refer patients to dermatologists effectively, particularly in settings with skin-of-color where BCC is uncommon and dermatological training is inadequate.
important_remarks: The study is published as a 'Correspondence' rather than a full research article, which may limit the depth of methodological reporting.
There is a significant conflict of interest: two authors are direct beneficiaries of profits from the app, and one is a salaried employee of the developing company (Nurithm Labs Private Limited).

The training dataset (17,784 images) was sourced from public archives (HellenicDermAtlas, Danderm) and Indian dermatologists, potentially introducing geographic and demographic bias regarding skin types and image quality.
The evaluation dataset was small (n=100 images: 58 BCC, 42 non-BCC), which may limit the statistical power and generalizability of the performance metrics.
The comparison group included only 14 non-dermatologists (5 surgeons, 9 general physicians), a very small sample size for robust statistical comparison.

The study did not report data leakage risks explicitly, though it states study images were independent of training images; however, the small evaluation set raises concerns about overfitting or selection bias.

The app's sensitivity (80.24%) was significantly higher than dermatologists (45.98%), but the high standard deviation in dermatologist performance (±21.21%) suggests high inter-observer variability among experts.
The app's specificity (91.57%) was comparable to dermatologists (96.03%) but lower than non-dermatologists (98.47%), indicating a trade-off between sensitivity and specificity.
The study focused on facial lesions only, limiting the applicability of the app to non-facial BCC presentations.

The app was tested on 'ordinary smartphones' by three different investigators, suggesting variability in hardware and user interaction was considered, but results were aggregated without detailed breakdown by device or user.

The study compares a binary/multi-class AI diagnosis against human experts making specific diagnoses, which is a more clinically relevant comparison than binary benign/malignant tasks used in some prior studies.
